title: Chapter 22: The Reckoning
content: I've been on the floor for six hours.

Not the couch. Not the bed. The floor — specifically the stretch of hardwood between the kitchen island and the window, where the late-afternoon light should be hitting the walnut Ikea bookshelf I impulse-bought in 2024 but is instead pooling golden and soft like a streetlamp on the Île Saint-Louis. Paris light. Through my Murray Hill window. Which faces a brick wall.
The apartment bleeds constantly now.

The smell hits in waves — salt and kelp and that specific cold-clean note of the Irish Sea, rolling through my kitchen like the tide. Underneath it: butter and thyme from a bistro on the Rue de Rivoli that doesn't exist in Manhattan. My radiator clicks the way the pipes in the Cork flat used to click, rhythm like a heartbeat, like a countdown I can't name.

On the kitchen counter behind me, ghost-cat pawprints appear one by one in a ring of spilled flour I never spilled. Small. Deliberate. A cat that lives in Timeline C, walking through my counter like it's her territory too.
I'm living in all three worlds and none of them at the same time.

My phone is face-down on the floor three feet away. I can't look at it. The integrity number is there. I watched it drop from 23% to 19% somewhere over the Atlantic, and I don't know if the decline is linear or exponential and I don't want to know because knowing means deciding what to do about it and I don't know what to do about it and—
The buzzer cuts through the salt-air like a knife.
I don't move. It buzzes again. Longer. Insistent.
Then: "Avery. It's me. Let me up or I'm calling the fire department to break down your door, I swear to God."
Mika.

I must have called her. I must have — there's a call log, probably, a timestamp, proof that my hands did something my brain doesn't remember authorizing. I drag myself upright. The world tilts. Not a glitch — just six hours horizontal after who knows how many hours of interdimensional travel. My legs are pins and needles from hip to heel.
I buzz her in. Open the door. Lean against the frame.

Mika Torres stands in my hallway wearing a paint-splattered denim jacket and an expression that says she prepared herself for worse and still isn't ready. Her eyes move over me — the wrinkled clothes, the hair I haven't brushed since Paris, the thousand-yard stare — and I wait for it. The "I told you so." The "You look like death." The "What the hell happened to you."
She says: "When's the last time you ate?"
I open my mouth. Close it. Truly cannot remember.
"Sit down," she says. "I'm making soup."

She doesn't ask where the ingredients are. She finds them. Mika in a kitchen is a force of nature — cabinet doors opening, the clatter of a pot, the hiss of olive oil hitting heat. I sit on the floor again because the couch feels too far and too permanent, like committing to a location means committing to a reality, and I can't commit to anything right now.
The smell of soup replaces the Irish Sea. Garlic. Onion. Chicken broth rising to a simmer. Real. Anchor-real. My stomach cramps and I realize I'm starving.

Fifteen minutes. Mika ladles soup into a mug — a mug, not a bowl, because she knows I can't handle a bowl right now, a bowl is a meal and a meal is normal and nothing about this is normal — and sets it on the floor beside me. She sits down. Cross-legged. Her shoulder an inch from mine.
She waits.

I drink the soup. It's too hot and I don't care. The warmth moves through me like proof of being alive, radiating from my chest outward, and maybe it's the first real thing I've felt in hours or maybe it's just chicken soup but my eyes burn and I blink hard and set the mug down.
"Avery." Her voice is quiet. "Talk to me."
So I do.

Not the half version from before. Not the safe, edited, this-could-be-a-psych-break confession I gave her in the café that time. The whole thing. The app I downloaded on a hangover whim that turned out to be a literal fork in reality. The three timelines. The three Julians — Julian A in Paris, beautiful and sure, who took me to the Pont des Arts and kissed me like I was the only woman in any universe. Julian B in New York, my coworker, hiding something enormous behind those measured smiles. Julian C in Cork, drowning in his father's debts, whose eyes had two men in them when I left.

The integrity percentage. The glitches — my hands flickering like bad reception, the way I can taste wine I never drank, the phantom bruises from injuries sustained in bodies that aren't quite mine. The merge. God, the merge — forcing Julian A's tactical mind into Julian C so he could outmaneuver Harker's men, feeling him become two people in the space of a single breath.
I pick up my phone. Turn it over.
INTEGRITY: 19%

Mika stares at the screen for a long time. The soup bubbles on the stove. The Paris light shifts through my brick-wall window. A ghost-cat pawprint appears on the counter, and I watch Mika notice it, watch her expression flicker from confusion to recognition to something like horror.
"You can see that," I say. Not a question.
"The pawprint? Yeah." She sets the phone down carefully, like it might bite. "Aves. How long has it been doing that?"
"Bleeding through? A while. It's getting worse."
She's quiet. I brace for the questions I can't answer, the skepticism I probably deserve. Instead, she says the thing I needed to hear and didn't know I needed:
"You're not crazy. This is real. But you're also destroying yourself, and you need to stop."
My throat closes. "Mika—"

"I'm serious. Look at you." Her hand finds my shoulder, firm and warm and here. "You're sitting on the floor of your apartment that smells like three different cities. You haven't eaten in God knows how long. Your integrity — whatever that means, whatever that number is — it's at nineteen percent. That's an F, Avery. That's a failing grade in being a person."
A laugh hiccups out of me. Wet. Unsteady. "It's not exactly a class I can withdraw from."
"No. But you can stop taking extra credit." Her grip tightens. "You can stop choosing to go."

My phone lights up. Julian's name. That photo I took of him on the balcony in Paris, wind in his hair, caught mid-laugh. The ringtone — the one I set thinking it was cute, thinking this was just a love story in one city.
I answer. "Julian."
"Avery." His voice is tight. Wound. "Where did you go? You disappeared from Paris. One second you were there. The next — gone. Like you were never there."
There's no good way to say it. "I went to Ireland."

A long silence. In it, I hear the echo of a man recalibrating his entire understanding of reality. The café sounds of Paris behind him — silverware, conversation, the hum of a city at its evening rhythm — and the weight of what he's not saying.
Then: "Is everyone okay?"
Not *are you okay*. Is *everyone*.

He's already thinking in multiverse. Already accepted that the woman he loves exists in more than one place, that her absence from his timeline means her presence in another, that checking on her means checking on all of her. It should make me feel less alone. It makes me feel like crying.
"I don't know how to fix this," I say.
"Then stop trying to fix it alone." No hesitation. Julian A doesn't do hesitation. "Come to the office. We'll figure it out."

I want to argue. I want to say you can't fix this with strategic thinking and quarterly reports. I want to say the app doesn't care about your five-year plan. But my throat is too full of soup-warmth and Mika's hand on my shoulder and the sound of a man who just learned his girlfriend can vanish between heartbeats, asking about everyone in her life instead of just himself.
"Okay," I say. "I'm coming."
I hang up. Mika's hand drops from my shoulder.

I stand. Or try to — my legs fold, and I catch the counter, and the ghost-pawprints smear under my palm like they were never there. I try again. Upright. Functional. A person leaving her apartment like a person does.
Mika blocks the doorway.

"Aves." Serious face. No-nonsense voice. The one she uses when I'm about to do something monumentally stupid and she knows she can't stop me but she's going to try anyway. "You can't keep doing this."
"I know—"
"Every time you shift, you lose a piece of yourself. You said the integrity was at nineteen percent." She holds up my phone. I didn't even see her pick it up. "What happens when it hits zero?"

I don't know. That's the truth. I don't know because Fork doesn't have a manual and there's no FAQ for "what happens when your consciousness frays past the point of coherence" and the app just shows the number like a battery draining on a phone you can't charge.
"I don't know," I say.
"Promise me you'll think about that before you shift again."
I hesitate.

The hesitation is the giveaway. Mika reads it instantly — the way my jaw tightens, the way my eyes flick toward the window, toward the direction of Sterling Capital, toward Julian and the promise of someone else shouldering this for five goddamn minutes. She knows I can't promise. Not when Julian C is still in danger, still in Cork with his father's ghosts and the men who want to break him. Not when Julian B's secret is coiled like a spring in our shared office, one bad day from detonating. Not when Harvey is circling my career like a shark, waiting for me to slip so he can take the whole thing down.
"I'll think about it," I say.

It's not a promise. It's a compromise, and we both know it. Mika's expression shifts — not anger, something worse. Resignation. The look of someone watching a movie where the protagonist makes the wrong choice and she can't stop the projector.
"I'll be here," she says. Steps aside. "When you need me. Again."

The walk to Sterling Capital is twelve blocks. I've done it a hundred times — past the bodega with the cat, past the dry cleaner that always gets my name wrong, past the brownstone with the window boxes that change with the seasons. Familiar. Manhattan-solid. Mine.
I get four blocks before the city glitches.
It's not a flicker. Not the micro-hesitations I've been managing for weeks, the momentary double-vision, the here-and-gone déjà vu. This is hard. This is total.
For three full seconds, Manhattan is gone.

I'm standing on cobblestones. Wet cobblestones, rain-slick and gleaming under a gray Irish sky, and the English Market rises in front of me — the arched entrance, the smell of fresh bread and ocean salt, a woman in a green raincoat pushing past me on my left. My heels are wrong for this street. My whole self is wrong for this street. I'm wearing Manhattan shoes on Cork cobblestones and the stone is real under my feet, solid, cold through the leather—

Paris. The Rue de Rivoli. Car horns and café chairs and the specific golden quality of light that only exists in that city in that hour, and someone is speaking French two feet away and I can smell the Seine—
Then void.

Nothing. No city. No cobblestones. No light. White noise — not silence, silence is empty, this is full, a wall of sound without frequency, without source, pressing against my eardrums from the inside. And the falling. The sense that the ground is gone and I'm dropping through empty space, not physically, not in my body, my *self* is falling, fraying out in long threads—
Manhattan snaps back.

I'm on the sidewalk. Hands and knees. The concrete is rough under my palms, real, present, here. My breath comes in heaves. A woman — forties, Lululemon, a small dog in a carrier — stops three feet away.
"Are you okay?"
I nod. Can't speak. My tongue is heavy and wrong in my mouth.
She hesitates. The dog whimpers. "Do you need me to call someone?"
"I'm fine." The words scrape out. "Low blood sugar. I'm fine."
She doesn't believe me. But New York is a city that respects the fiction of being fine, so she walks on. The dog looks back at me over her shoulder. I watch them turn the corner.
Then I look at my phone.
The screen is lit. I didn't unlock it. I didn't touch it. It's displaying the Fork app — the interface I didn't open, the numbers I didn't ask for.
INTEGRITY: 15%
And below it, in red text I've never seen before:
**AUTONOMOUS SHIFT DETECTED**

The words don't make sense. Autonomous. Without external control. Self-governing. The app is shifting me without permission. The worlds aren't waiting for me to choose anymore — they're coming to me, pulling me, tearing through whichever reality is closest like currents through a river that used to run in one direction.
I'm on my feet. I don't remember standing. The sidewalk is solid. The bodega cat watches me from the window. The brownstone window boxes are full of geraniums. Everything is exactly Manhattan.
But I didn't choose to come back.

I get to Sterling Capital. Julian is waiting. But under his voice and his certainty and the strategic mind that thinks in contingency plans, under Mika's soup-warmth still lingering in my chest, a cold question pulses:
*What happens at zero?*
I don't know. And for the first time, I'm not the one driving toward the answer.
